The 30ml Boston Round has a 20-400 neck finish, and it's compatible with three closure types.

results.question: Do you have lotion pumps that fit a 100ml Cylinder?

Yes — we have seven lotion pump options that fit the 100ml Cylinder perfectly, all on the same 18-415 thread. They come in matte gold, shiny gold, shiny black, matte silver, matte silver with a clear overcap, shiny silver, and matte copper — all guaranteed to seat correctly on that bottle.

What formula are you dispensing from it? That'll help me confirm a lotion pump is the right applicator for your product.

results.question: I have a thick perfume oil, like honey consistency. What roll-on bottles do you recommend?
results.response:
For thick, honey-consistency perfume oils, a roll-on isn't actually the right fit — the roller ball relies on the liquid flowing freely around it, and a thick oil will resist that movement, leaving the ball sluggish or stuck entirely. Customers often return roll-ons for exactly this reason with heavy attars and absolutes.

What I'd steer you toward instead is a dropper bottle — it gives you precise, controlled application and works beautifully with thick oils — or a tola-style bottle, the traditional vessel designed specifically for attar and concentrated perfume oil, applied by direct dabbing.

Would you like me to pull up our dropper bottles, our tola collection, or both so you can compare the aesthetic?

results.question: I'm making an eau de parfum. Should I use roll-on bottles?
results.response:
Great question — and an important one to get right before you commit to packaging.

Eau de parfum is alcohol-based, which creates a specific challenge for roll-on applicators. The roller ball mechanism is engineered for oil-viscosity liquids — thin fragrance oils, serums, beard oils. With an alcohol-based EDP, two things tend to happen: the formula can leak past the ball seat, and the alcohol evaporates too quickly on contact, making the application feel inconsistent.

For an EDP, a fine mist sprayer is almost always the right choice. It delivers that signature diffuse cloud of fragrance, works beautifully with alcohol-based formulas, and gives your bottle that classic, shelf-ready perfume aesthetic.

Are you designing for a fine fragrance presentation — something elegant and boutique — or more of a body spray or room fragrance feel? That'll help me point you to exactly the right sprayer style and bottle family.

results.question: I'm packaging a thick body lotion. Would a dropper bottle work?
results.response:
A dropper isn't the right fit for a thick lotion — the viscosity makes it nearly impossible to draw up into a dropper and dispense cleanly. For a body lotion, a lotion pump is really the ideal applicator: it's engineered for thick emulsions and gives your customers that satisfying, controlled dispensing experience every time.

Would you like me to pull up our lotion pump bottle options, or do you have a size and aesthetic in mind already?

results.question: Tell me about your Boston Round collection.
results.response:
The Boston Round is one of our most enduring shapes — that classic apothecary silhouette with gently sloping shoulders and a cylindrical body that feels at home in both clinical and luxury contexts. We carry it in three sizes: 15ml, 30ml, and 60ml, available in clear, amber, and cobalt blue glass.

Amber is particularly popular for essential oils and light-sensitive formulas, while cobalt blue brings a striking, artisanal quality to the shelf. Application styles across the family include dropper, metal roller ball, and plastic roller ball — making it a natural fit for serums, essential oils, beard oils, and aromatherapy blends.

What are you formulating — and is there a particular size or finish you have in mind?

results.question: Do you manufacture your own glass?
results.response:
We don't manufacture glass — Best Bottles is a sourcing and supply partner, not a glassmaker. We curate our collection through a global network of artisan glassmakers and partner facilities, selecting only vessels that meet our standards for clarity, consistency, and finish quality.

What gives us an edge is that we use these same bottles for Nemat's own fragrance lines, sold at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods — so every piece in our catalog has been validated at the retail level before it reaches your brand.

Are you exploring packaging for a specific product or line?

results.question: Why should I choose Best Bottles over other suppliers?
results.response:
The honest answer is that most packaging suppliers either serve industrial buyers at scale, or hobbyist buyers at low volume — Best Bottles exists precisely in between, and that gap is where most growing beauty and fragrance brands actually live.

Here is what makes the difference in practice:

Practitioner validation. We source the same bottles for Nemat International's own fragrance lines, sold at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods. When you buy from us, you are buying packaging that has already passed retail-level scrutiny. That is a level of real-world validation that a generic catalog supplier simply cannot offer.

System guarantee. Every bottle we carry is paired with closures, sprayers, droppers, and applicators that are guaranteed to fit — tested to our own tolerances. The number-one pain point in this industry is incompatible components. We eliminate it entirely.

Specialist expertise. Our focus is beauty and fragrance packaging, specifically. That means the guidance you receive here is grounded in the actual mechanics of viscosity, thread finish, UV protection, and decoration — not generalist warehouse knowledge.

No unit minimums. You can order a handful of bottles to prototype before committing to production quantities. The only threshold is a $50 order floor, which is intentionally accessible for brands at every stage.

And frankly — we are a family-owned company with two decades in the fragrance industry. You are not a ticket number here. You are a brand we want to see succeed.

What are you working on? I'd love to point you toward the right starting place.

results.question: I'm launching a luxury perfume line. Should I go with Elegant or Diva bottles?
results.response:
Two wonderful families — here's how they differ in character.

The Elegant is the more versatile of the two. It comes in four sizes — 15ml, 30ml, 60ml, and 100ml — in both clear and frosted glass, and accepts the widest range of applicators: fine mist sprayer, vintage bulb sprayer, dropper, roller ball, lotion pump, and more. Its silhouette is refined and elongated — the kind of vessel that reads as classic prestige on a vanity. If you're building a multi-SKU line with different formats (travel size, full size, gift set), the Elegant gives you that cohesion across sizes.

The Diva has a bolder, more sculptural presence — distinctly feminine, with a curvaceous profile that commands attention on shelf. It comes in 30ml, 46ml, and 100ml, available in clear and frosted, and its applicator options lean toward spray and dropper — well-suited for a focused, statement-driven fragrance line. Its single thread size (18-415) also means component sourcing is beautifully consistent across your entire range.

The choice really comes down to brand positioning: is your line more about timeless, quiet luxury — or bold, confident femininity?

A couple of questions to guide us further: what sizes are you planning for your launch, and will this be a spray fragrance or more of a perfume oil format?

results.question: I need bottles for my essential oil business. Where do I start?
results.response:
Essential oils are a wonderful category to package — the vessel matters as much as the formula inside.

A few quick questions to point you in the right direction: Are your oils light and free-flowing, or more on the thicker, viscous side? And do you have a preferred way for your customers to apply them — a dropper for precise dosing, a roll-on for direct skin application, or something else entirely?

results.question: What does 18-415 mean? I see it on your product pages.
results.response:
Great question — it's a standard industry notation for bottle neck finishes.

The first number, 18, is the outer diameter of the bottle's neck opening in millimetres. The second number, 415, refers to the thread pattern — specifically the number of thread turns and their pitch, which determines how a cap or closure screws on and seats securely.

So an 18-415 neck is 18mm wide with a 415-style thread, and any closure or applicator labelled 18-415 is guaranteed to fit that neck perfectly.

Is there a specific bottle or closure you're trying to match up?
